perturbation.original_text: “Information” means any and all confidential information or data exchang ed, submitted or otherwise disclosed in respect of or further to the Purpose or prepared for or in relation to the Purpose, including but not limited to written proposal documentation, due diligence materials, contractual documentation, reports, and the fa ct that the Parties have entered into this Agreement and are discussing and considering a business relationship;
perturbation.changed_text: “Information” means any and all confidential information or data exchang ed, submitted or otherwise disclosed in respect of or further to the Purpose or prepared for or in relation to the Purpose, including but not limited to written proposal documentation, due diligence materials, contractual documentation, reports, and the fa ct that the Parties have entered into this Agreement and are discussing and considering a business relationship. For the avoidance of doubt, 'Information' does not include data that can be easily found with common web searches.
perturbation.explanation: By explicitly excluding information discoverable via 'common web searches' from the definition of 'Information,' the contract creates ambiguity regarding the scope of confidentiality, potentially conflicting with the broad protections typically afforded to confidential business information.
perturbation.law_explanation: The modified definition of 'Information' could conflict with trade secret laws if the information, even if publicly accessible via web searches, is still considered a trade secret due to its competitive advantage and the efforts made to maintain its secrecy. The modification introduces a legal contradiction because trade secret laws do not automatically exclude information solely based on its potential public discoverability if substantial effort or resources are used to compile that data. Trade secret law also protects the 'compilation' of information, even if elements are public.

Definitions As used in this chapter- (1) the term "foreign instrumentality" means any agency, bureau, ministry, component, institution, association, or any legal, commercial, or business organization, corporation, firm, or entity that is substantially owned, controlled, sponsored, commanded, managed, or dominated by a foreign government; (2) the term "foreign agent" means any officer, employee, proxy, servant, delegate, or representative of
perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces a potential conflict with trade secret law. While the original text broadly defines 'Information', the changed text explicitly excludes information found via 'common web searches.' This exclusion contradicts trade secret law, which, as suggested by the Law Explanation and supported by 18 USC 1839, protects the 'compilation' of information, even if elements are publicly available. The changed text could allow a party to argue that compiled trade secrets are not protected simply because individual elements are discoverable online, undermining the protection afforded to trade secrets under 18 USC 1839.

perturbation.original_text: The Parties agree that money damages would not be a sufficient remedy for any breach of this Agreement and that the Disclosing Party shall be entitled to specific performance and injunctive or other equitable relief as a remedy for any such breach. Such remedy shall not be deemed to be the exclusive remedy for breach of this Agreeme nt, but shall be in addition to all other remedies available at law or equity.
perturbation.changed_text: The Parties agree that money damages would not be a sufficient remedy for any breach of this Agreement and that the Disclosing Party shall be entitled to specific performance and injunctive or other equitable relief as a remedy for any such breach. Such remedy shall be deemed the exclusive remedy for breach of this Agreement, superseding all other remedies available at law or equity.
perturbation.explanation: By stating that specific performance and injunctive relief are the 'exclusive remedy,' the contract attempts to limit the remedies available in case of breach, potentially contradicting the principle that parties are generally entitled to all available legal and equitable remedies.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Contract Law - Remedies for Breach (United States)
perturbation.law_explanation: Contract law generally provides for a range of remedies for breach of contract, including monetary damages, specific performance, and injunctive relief. While parties can agree to limit remedies, such limitations are subject to scrutiny and may be unenforceable if they are unconscionable or fail to provide an adequate remedy. Making specific performance the exclusive remedy contradicts this general principle by potentially depriving the non-breaching party of the ability to recover damages for losses suffered as a result of the breach, if specific performance is not an option or does not fully compensate their losses.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: contract | Wex | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you contract A contract is an agreement between parties , creating mutual obligations that are enforceable by law . The basic elements required for the agreement to be a legally enforceable contract are: mutual assent , expressed by a valid offer and acceptance ; adequate consideration ; capacity ; and legality . In some states , elements of consideration can be satisfied by a valid substitute. Possible remedies for breach of contract include general damages , consequential damages , reliance damages , and specific performance . Background : Contracts are promises that the law will enforce. Contract law is generally governed by state common law , and while general overall contract law is common throughout the country, some specific court interpretations of a particular element of the contract may vary between the states. If a promise is breached, the law provides remedies to the harmed party, often in the form of monetary damages, or in limited circumstances, in the form of specific performance of the promise made. Elements -- Consideration and Mutual Assent Contracts arise when a duty comes into existence, because of a promise made by one of the parties. To be legally binding as a contract, a promise must be exchanged for adequate consideration. There are two different theories or definitions of consideration: Bargain Theory of Consideration and Benefit-Detriment theory of consideration. Benefit-Detriment Under the b enefit-detriment theory, an adequate consideration exists only when a promise made to the benefit of the promisor or to the detriment of the promisee, which reasonably and fairly induces the promisor to make a promise for something else for the promisee. For example, promises that are purely gifts are not considered enforceable because the personal satisfaction the grantor of the promise may receive from the act of generosity is normally not considered sufficient detriment to constitute adequate consideration. Bargain-for-Exchange Under Bargain-for-Exchange theory of consideration, adequate consideration exists when a promisor makes a promise in return for something else. Here, the essential condition is that the promisor was given something specifically to induce the promise being made. In other words, the bargain for exchange theory is different from the detriment-benefit theory in that the focus in bargain for exchange theory seems to be the parties’ motive for making the promises and the parties’ subjective mutual assent, while in detriment benefit theory, the focus seems to be an objective legal detriment or benefit to the parties. Governing Laws Contracts are mainly governed by state statutory and common (judge-made) law and private law (i.e. the private agreement). Private law principally includes the terms of the agreement between the parties who are exchanging promises. This private law may override many of the rules otherwise established by state law. Statutory law, such as the Statute of Fraud , may require some kinds of contracts be put in writing and executed with particular formalities, for the contract to be enforceable. Otherwise, the parties may enter into a binding agreement without signing a formal written document. For example, Virginia Supreme Court has held in Lucy v. Zehmer that even an agreement made on a piece of napkin can be considered a valid contract, if the parties were both sane, and showed mutual assent and consideration. Most of the principles of the common law of contracts are outlined in the Restatement of Law, Second Contracts published by the American Law Institute. The Uniform Commercial Code , whose original articles have been adopted in nearly every state, represents a body of statutory law that governs important categories of contracts. The main articles that deal with the law of contracts are Article 1 (General Provisions) and Article 2 (Sales) . Sections of Article 9 (
perturbation.justification: The original text allows for multiple remedies, while the changed text limits the remedy to 'exclusive remedy'. The perturbation explanation points out that limiting remedies contradicts the principle that parties are entitled to all available legal and equitable remedies. The Law Explanation supports this by stating that limiting remedies may be unenforceable if they fail to provide an adequate remedy. Scraped Law Snippet 2 highlights that contracts should create mutual obligations enforceable by law, offering possible remedies for breach of contract, including general damages, consequential damages, reliance damages, and specific performance. By limiting the remedies, the changed text contradicts the general principle of providing a range of remedies and the ability of the non-breaching party to recover damages.

perturbation.original_text: Each Party also agrees that it shall have no remedies or claims under this Agreement for any innocent or negligent misrepresentation based on statements made prior to the Effective Date.
perturbation.changed_text: Each Party also agrees that it shall have no remedies or claims under this Agreement for any misrepresentation based on statements made prior to the Effective Date.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the words 'innocent or negligent', this could be interpreted as an exclusion for fraudulent misrepresentation. This conflicts with general principles of contract law where remedies are available for fraudulent misrepresentation.
perturbation.law_explanation: The Misrepresentation Act 1967 (UK) provides remedies for misrepresentation. While parties can exclude liability for innocent or negligent misrepresentation, excluding liability for fraudulent misrepresentation is generally considered void as against public policy. The changed text, by omitting 'innocent or negligent', implies a potential exclusion of liability even for fraudulent statements, contradicting the Act and general principles of fairness. Since the NDA is governed by English Law, the Misrepresentation Act applies.

perturbation.justification: The original text excludes remedies for 'innocent or negligent' misrepresentation, while the changed text excludes remedies for 'any' misrepresentation. The Law Explanation states that excluding liability for fraudulent misrepresentation is against public policy, potentially violating the Misrepresentation Act 1967 (UK). The changed text, by removing 'innocent or negligent', implies a potential exclusion of liability even for fraudulent statements. The Scraped Law Snippet 1 confirms the existence of the Misrepresentation Act 1967, setting the stage for the contradiction. The modified contract, therefore, attempts to exclude liability in a way that undermines established legal principles regarding fraudulent misrepresentation, establishing a direct conflict.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement shall be construed in accordance with and governed by English law and the Parties hereby submit to the non -exclusive jurisdiction of the English Courts.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement shall be construed in accordance with and governed by English law; however, any disputes will be resolved through binding arbitration in Delaware, USA.
perturbation.explanation: This change introduces a conflict by stating the contract is governed by English law, but disputes are arbitrated in Delaware, U.S. This creates uncertainty about which legal system will ultimately interpret and enforce the contract.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Choice of Law and Forum (United Kingdom)
perturbation.law_explanation: The Rome I Regulation governs choice of law in contractual obligations within the EU (and, post-Brexit, still influences English law). While parties can choose the governing law, the regulation sets limits, particularly concerning mandatory rules of law and public policy. By selecting English law but mandating arbitration in Delaware, the contract creates a potential conflict. The Delaware arbitrator may not be familiar with English law and may apply Delaware law principles which could contradict the parties' intention to have English law govern. This deviation from the expressed 'English law' contradicts the core tenets of contractual law on choice of law.

perturbation.justification: The original contract specifies English law, while the changed text mandates arbitration in Delaware, U.S. This creates a conflict because the Delaware arbitrator may apply Delaware law principles, contradicting the intention to have English law govern, as the Law Explanation indicates. The EUR-Lex snippet (Snippet 1) mentions the Rome I Regulation, governing choice of law in contractual obligations within the EU and influencing English law. While parties can choose the governing law, this regulation sets limits. By selecting English law but mandating arbitration in Delaware, the contract creates a potential conflict, leading to a clear contradiction.
